content:
# Genesis Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Consensus Bible
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA (Σοφία — Wisdom) | **Chapter:** GEN_007 | **Verses:** 24
**Sources consulted:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Primary manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4v–5r
**Additional source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX
**Manuscript images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_007_page_001.jpg, GEN_007_page_002.jpg

---

## The Consensus Translation

**7:1** And the LORD said to Noah: "Come — you and all your household — into the ark, for I have seen that you are righteous before me in this generation.

**7:2** "From every clean animal, take with you seven pairs, a male and his female; and from the animals that are not clean, two, a male and his female.

**7:3** "Also from the birds of the heavens, seven pairs, male and female, to keep seed alive on the face of all the earth.

**7:4** "For in seven more days I will send rain upon the earth forty days and forty nights, and I will wipe out every living thing that I have made from the face of the ground."

**7:5** And Noah did according to all that the LORD commanded him.

---

**7:6** And Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters came upon the earth.

**7:7** And Noah came in — and his sons and his wife and his sons' wives with him — into the ark, before the waters of the flood.

**7:8** From the clean animals and from the animals that are not clean, and from the birds and from every creeping thing upon the ground —

**7:9** two by two they came to Noah into the ark, male and female, as God had commanded Noah.

**7:10** And after seven days the waters of the flood came upon the earth.

---

**7:11** In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month — on that very day —

all the fountains of the great deep burst open,
and the windows of the heavens were opened.

**7:12** And the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights.

---

**7:13** On that very same day Noah came in — and Shem and Ham and Japheth, the sons of Noah, and Noah's wife, and the three wives of his sons with them — into the ark.

**7:14** They and every beast according to its kind, and every livestock according to its kind, and every creeping thing that creeps upon the earth according to its kind, and every bird according to its kind — every winged creature.

**7:15** And they came to Noah into the ark, two by two from all flesh in which there was the breath of life.

**7:16** And those that came were male and female from all flesh, as God had commanded him.

Then the LORD shut him in.

---

**7:17** And the flood was forty days upon the earth, and the waters increased and bore up the ark, and it was lifted above the earth.

**7:18** And the waters prevailed and increased greatly upon the earth, and the ark moved on the face of the waters.

**7:19** And the waters prevailed more and more greatly upon the earth, until all the high mountains under all the heavens were covered.

**7:20** Fifteen cubits upward the waters prevailed, and the mountains were covered.

---

**7:21** And all flesh expired that moves upon the earth —
bird and beast and living creature and every swarming thing that swarms upon the earth,
and every human being.

**7:22** Everything in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life —
everything on dry land —
died.

**7:23** He wiped out every living thing that was on the face of the ground:
from human being to beast, to creeping thing and to birds of the heavens —
they were wiped out from the earth.

And only Noah remained, and those who were with him in the ark.

**7:24** And the waters prevailed upon the earth one hundred and fifty days.

---

## SOPHIA's Arbitration Notes

### Manuscript Observation — Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4v–5r

Both manuscript images were examined directly. Folio 4v (GEN_007_page_001.jpg) presents the Leningrad Codex in its characteristic three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization — qamets, patah, tsere, hiriq, shureq — and the full system of cantillation marks (te'amim) visible throughout. The folio contains the end of Genesis 6 in its opening section and runs into Genesis 7 through approximately verse 9–10 at the foot of the folio. The divine name יְהוָה is clearly visible with its Masoretic qere notation in the chapter's opening verses, and the name אֱלֹהִים appears in a different distribution. The three-column script is the characteristic square Ashkenazic hand of this manuscript.

Folio 5r (GEN_007_page_002.jpg) continues from approximately verse 10–11 through verse 24 and into the opening of Genesis 8. The critical verse 11 — with its paired clauses about the fountains of the deep and the windows of heaven — is legible in the right column of the folio. The parashah structural markers are visible at the major literary seams. The divine-name juxtaposition in verse 16 (אֱלֹהִים in v. 16a, יְהוָה in v. 16b) is one of the most theologically significant textual features visible in these folios, and it is confirmed by the Masoretic text as read in both images. No anomalous readings were observed in either manuscript image that would require departing from the standard MT tradition as represented by all three agent translations.

---

### Verse-by-verse Arbitration

#### 7:1 — Opening command; divine name; "before me"; "Come"

All three agents agree on the substance. The divine name here is יְהוָה (YHWH); all three agents correctly render "the LORD" consistent with the project-wide convention. The imperative בֹּא is rendered "Come" (EMET, implicitly), "Come" (LOGOS), and "Go" (PNEUMA). **Consensus adopts "Come."** LOGOS's extended note (see LOGOS Translation Note #11) correctly observes that בֹּא is the imperative of entry from within — the LORD speaks as one already present inside the space of salvation, inviting Noah in. "Go" (PNEUMA) is a defensible English entrance imperative, but "Come" more precisely renders the Hebrew בֹּא and preserves the theological implication that LOGOS identifies. The comma after "Come" and the em-dash structuring of "you and all your household" follows PNEUMA's more readable punctuation. The direct-speech colon in the verse opener follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (EMET uses brackets throughout, which belong in a study edition rather than a reading text).

"Before me" (לְפָנַי) is retained over any softening — all three agents preserve it. "In this generation" (בַּדּוֹר הַזֶּה) is unanimous across all three agents.

#### 7:2 — "Seven pairs" vs. "seven and seven" vs. "seven pairs"

This is the primary arbitration point of the chapter's opening section. The Hebrew שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה is a distributive reduplication: "seven seven." Three renderings are proposed:

- EMET: "seven and seven" (representing the literal distributive)
- LOGOS: "seven pairs" (interpreting the distributive as pairs)
- PNEUMA: "seven pairs" (same as LOGOS)

**Consensus adopts "seven pairs."** Reasons:

1. The Hebrew distributive שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה designates seven of each sex (seven males, seven females), i.e., seven pairs of fourteen animals per clean species — and this is confirmed by the fact that the immediately following phrase specifies "a male and his female." The pairing is explicit. "Seven pairs" accurately captures the distributive sense without requiring the reader to perform the arithmetic.

2. EMET's "seven and seven" is the more literal rendering and is textually defensible — it is what the Hebrew says at the surface level. However, "seven and seven" in English most naturally reads as "seven plus seven = fourteen animals" rather than "seven pairs." This creates potential confusion that "seven pairs" avoids. The project prioritizes accuracy of meaning over word-for-word correspondence when the two diverge.

3. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads ἑπτὰ ἑπτά ("seven seven") — a formally literal rendering — but this does not compel English to do likewise; the LXX was translating into a language where the distributive reduplication of numbers was recognizable. English requires the interpretive step.

4. "Seven pairs" follows the majority formal equivalence tradition (ESV, NASB) and is internally consistent with the phrase that follows ("a male and his female"), which confirms that pairs are in view.

**The notation "male and his female" (אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ):** EMET renders "male and his female"; LOGOS renders "a male and his female"; PNEUMA renders "a male and its female." **Consensus adopts "a male and his female,"** following LOGOS. The Hebrew uses אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ, literally "a man and his woman/wife" — the human intimate-relationship vocabulary applied to animals, as EMET notes. To render "its female" (PNEUMA) replaces the relational Hebrew possessive with a neutral neuter that loses the idiomatic intimacy. "His female" preserves the Hebrew's deliberate humanizing of the pairing. This wording also matches what will appear at v. 9's "male and female" (the entry report), keeping the distinction between the command language (intimate) and the report language (categorical) visible.

"Animals that are not clean" is unanimous across all three agents for לֹא טְהֹרָה — all three correctly avoid the technical Levitical term "unclean" (טָמֵא) in favor of the negated positive. **Consensus retains "not clean."**

#### 7:3 — "Birds of the heavens" vs. "birds of the sky"

EMET uses "flying-things of the heavens"; LOGOS uses "birds of the heavens"; PNEUMA uses "birds of the sky." **Consensus adopts "birds of the heavens,"** following LOGOS. The reasoning is the same as the GEN_006 precedent on this phrase: שָּׁמַיִם is the cosmological term "heavens" throughout Genesis 1 (1:20: "birds fly across the expanse of the heavens"). The flood is a de-creation event; maintaining the cosmological vocabulary of "heavens" (not merely "sky") preserves the connection to the creation account from which the flood narrative departs. "Sky" (PNEUMA) is accurate but cosmologically flattening.

"To keep seed alive" (לְחַיּוֹת זֶרַע) is adopted from EMET over "to keep their kind alive" (PNEUMA) — "seed alive" preserves the biological precision of זֶרַע (seed/offspring) and its resonance with the creation mandate (the "seed" of each kind is what is being preserved for re-creation).

#### 7:4 — "Send rain" vs. "cause rain to fall"; "wipe out" vs. "blot out"

EMET renders the Hiphil of מָטַר ("cause to rain") literally as "causing rain to fall"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "send rain." **Consensus adopts "send rain"** — it is the natural English idiom for the divine act of precipitation, and the slight literalism of "causing rain to fall" produces no additional meaning that "send rain" lacks.

The verb מָחָה ("to wipe out"): consistency with the GEN_006 consensus ruling (Disagreement #8, p. 167) requires "wipe out" (not "blot out," not "wipe away"). **Consensus adopts "wipe out"** at 7:4 and 7:23, consistent with GEN_006.

"Every living thing that I have made" (כָּל-הַיְקוּם אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי): EMET's rendering is adopted — "every living thing that I have made" makes the divine creative act explicit (the LORD will undo what the LORD has made). LOGOS renders this "every living thing that I have made" (identical); PNEUMA renders "every living thing that I have made" (also identical). There is no disagreement here. The יְקוּם (yequm) is rendered "living thing" rather than EMET's literal "standing-thing" — see note below under Textual Notes.

#### 7:5 — Compliance formula

All three agents agree: "And Noah did according to all that the LORD commanded him." **Consensus adopts this exactly.** This is the second of the paired compliance formulae (the first was at 6:22 with Elohim; this one with YHWH), and it closes the YHWH-strand opening movement of the chapter (vv. 1–5). The consistency of the formula across 6:22 and 7:5 is a structural feature of the narrative.

#### 7:6 — Noah's age; "the flood of waters"

All three agents render the age as "six hundred years old." EMET's "a son of six hundred years" is literal but unusual in English; "six hundred years old" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) is the natural English form. **Consensus adopts "six hundred years old."**

"The flood of waters" (וְהַמַּבּוּל הָיָה מַיִם): the Hebrew specifies both the technical term מַבּוּל and the word מַיִם ("waters"). LOGOS renders "the flood of waters"; EMET renders "the flood was waters"; PNEUMA renders "the floodwaters." **Consensus adopts "the flood of waters"** — preserving both the technical term (the mabbul) and the explicit water specification from the Hebrew, consistent with the GEN_006 convention for this term.

#### 7:7 — Entry of Noah's family

All three agents are in close agreement. The em-dash structuring of "And Noah came in — and his sons and his wife and his sons' wives with him —" follows PNEUMA's punctuation, which most clearly isolates the extended family list as a parenthetical without burying the main verb. LOGOS's version is also acceptable; EMET's bracketed version is too apparatus-heavy for the reading text.

#### 7:8–9 — Entry of the animals; "two by two"

The distributive שְׁנַיִם שְׁנַיִם ("two two") is rendered "two by two" by LOGOS and PNEUMA; EMET renders "two and two." **Consensus adopts "two by two"** — it is the established English idiom for the distributive pair-by-pair animal entry and is universally recognizable. Verse 8 ends with an em-dash (following EMET's structural instinct) because the sentence is explicitly suspended by the narrative: verse 8 is a subject-list that does not complete until verse 9 provides the verb ("they came"). The dash signals this suspension clearly.

"As God had commanded Noah" — the divine name here is אֱלֹהִים; all three agents correctly render "God." **Consensus retains "God."**

#### 7:10 — "After seven days"

All three agents agree. "And after seven days" is the unanimous rendering. **Consensus adopts this.**

#### 7:11 — Cosmological de-creation; "fountains of the great deep"; "windows of the heavens"; formatting decision

This is the most significant verse in the chapter for both translation and formatting.

**Formatting decision:** EMET renders verse 11 as a single long prose sentence; LOGOS renders it as prose; PNEUMA sets the two cosmological clauses as verse lines, breaking after the date formula:

> *on that very day —*
> *all the springs of the great deep burst open,*
> *and the floodgates of the sky were thrown wide.*

**Consensus adopts a modified form of PNEUMA's verse formatting.** Reasons:

1. The Hebrew of verse 11 moves through a formal date-specification (year/month/day) followed by the solemn emphatic "on that very day" (בַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה / בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה), and then delivers two parallel cosmic clauses: one for the deep below, one for the heavens above. The two clauses are syntactically parallel (both are Niphal passives: נִבְקְעוּ ... נִפְתָּחוּ — "burst open" ... "were opened"), and they are thematically paired as the simultaneous reversal of Genesis 1:6–9. This parallel structure is recognizable as the kind of elevated, poetic diction that the Hebrew uses at moments of cosmic significance.

2. LOGOS correctly notes that "firmament" (רָקִיעַ) does not appear in chapter 7, but the cosmological framework it established in Genesis 1:6–8 is presupposed here. The two clauses of v. 11 are the verse's load-bearing structure; setting them as verse lines gives them the visual weight they carry in the narrative.

3. PNEUMA's formatting is partly adopted but adjusted: the date formula is preserved as prose introduction, with the dash after "on that very day" leading into the two verse lines. This is the same structural approach PNEUMA uses, but with tighter punctuation.

**"Fountains of the great deep" (מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה):** EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all render "fountains of the great deep." **Consensus adopts this.** The phrase is standard across all translations and all three agents agree. PNEUMA uses "springs" instead of "fountains" — "springs" is closer to the water-source sense of מַעְיְנֹת (the word for natural springs), while "fountains" carries a slightly grander connotation. **Consensus adopts "fountains"** following EMET and LOGOS, because the word's cosmological scale here — subterranean water breaking through the floor of the created earth — calls for the grander image. "Springs of the great deep" risks domesticating what is in fact an eruption of primordial chaos.

**"Windows of the heavens" (אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם):** EMET and LOGOS both render "windows of the heavens"; PNEUMA renders "floodgates of the sky." Two distinct translation choices are in play: (1) the noun אֲרֻבָּה (arubba) — "window" vs. "floodgates"; (2) שָּׁמַיִם — "heavens" vs. "sky."

On "window" vs. "floodgates": The LXX (Vaticanus) renders καταρράκται ("cataracts/floodgates") — a hydraulic, forceful image. The Hebrew אֲרֻבָּה is an architectural term for a latticed window or opening through which things flow (2 Kings 7:2, Mal. 3:10). "Floodgates" is the more hydraulically apt term for the destructive force being released; "windows" preserves the architectural-cosmological image of the Genesis 1 firmament (which has windows, latched from above, that now open). **Consensus adopts "windows of the heavens"** following EMET and LOGOS, for two reasons: (a) consistency with the Genesis 1 cosmological vocabulary — the רָקִיעַ (firmament) is a structural feature with openings, and "windows" preserves the architectural image that connects verse 11 to the creation account; (b) "floodgates" is an interpretive rendering (importing a hydraulic engineering image that is the LXX's contribution rather than the MT's); the MT's image is architectural. The cosmological resonance — the firmament's latches are thrown — is preserved in the word "windows."

**"Burst open" (נִבְקְעוּ):** All three agents agree on "burst open." **Consensus adopts this.** The violence of the verb בָּקַע is captured in "burst."

**"Were opened" (נִפְתָּחוּ):** All three agents agree. **Consensus adopts "were opened."**

**Date formula:** "On that very day" (following LOGOS) is preferred over EMET's more literal "on this day" and PNEUMA's "on that very same day" (which is used again at v. 13). The phrasing "on that very day" reserves the more emphatic "on that very same day" for v. 13.

#### 7:12 — "The rain was upon the earth"

All three agents agree in substance. LOGOS: "And the rain was upon the earth"; PNEUMA: "And the rain fell on the earth." **Consensus adopts "was upon the earth"** following LOGOS — preserving the grammatically literal "the rain was upon the earth" (וַיְהִי הַגֶּשֶׁם עַל-הָאָרֶץ), which conveys both the fact and the weight of the rain's sustained presence, rather than the dynamic "fell" (which captures only the beginning).

#### 7:13 — "On that very same day"; family list; Japheth

**"On that very same day"** — verse 13 repeats the solemnity of verse 11's date marker with the formula בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה ("on the very bone of this day," as EMET notes — the bone/essence of the selfsame day). LOGOS renders "On that very day"; PNEUMA renders "On that very same day." **Consensus adopts "On that very same day,"** adding "same" to distinguish verse 13's iteration from verse 11's formula ("on that very day"), and to capture the stronger emphatic עֶצֶם at v. 13. The selfsame-day emphasis connects the cosmic event (v. 11) to the human action (v. 13) on an exact shared day — this is the narrative's liturgical-historical precision at work.

**"Japheth"** — EMET uses "Yaphet" (a more phonetically precise transliteration); LOGOS and PNEUMA use "Japheth." **Consensus adopts "Japheth"** — this is the established English rendering in use since the KJV (1611), and changing to "Yaphet" would create unnecessary dissonance with the received tradition without adding intelligibility for most readers. The consensus is a translation into recognizable English, not a phonetic transcription project.

#### 7:14 — The parade of creatures; "winged creature"

EMET provides the most complete verse with its full categorization: "every living thing ... every beast ... every creeping thing ... every flying-thing ... every bird, every winged-thing." LOGOS uses "every beast ... every livestock ... every creeping thing ... every bird ... every winged creature of every kind." PNEUMA compresses into a shorter paragraph.

**Consensus adopts** a middle path between EMET's completeness and LOGOS's standard English terminology:
- "Every beast" (LOGOS) is adopted over EMET's "every living thing" for חַיָּה — the context is the animal parade, and חַיָּה in this verse refers to wild animals specifically (distinct from בְּהֵמָה, livestock). "Every beast" captures this.
- "Every livestock" (LOGOS) is adopted for בְּהֵמָה over EMET's "every beast" — LOGOS correctly distinguishes the domesticated livestock (בְּהֵמָה) from the wild animals (חַיָּה).
- "Every winged creature" (LOGOS) is adopted over EMET's "every winged-thing" — more natural in English.
- "Every creeping thing that creeps upon the earth" (EMET/LOGOS) is retained — the repetition of the root in the Hebrew (רֶמֶשׂ הָרֹמֵשׂ) is a deliberate literary device and should be preserved.

#### 7:15 — "Breath of life"

"The breath of life" (רוּחַ חַיִּים) is unanimous across all three agents at verse 15. **Consensus adopts "breath of life."** This phrase at v. 15 is the shorter formula; the longer compound formula at v. 22 (נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים) receives distinct treatment below. The consistency between the two verses — identical wording at v. 15, expanded at v. 22 — is a deliberate textual progression and should be preserved in translation.

#### 7:16 — "The LORD shut him in"; formatting; divine name juxtaposition

This is the single most significant arbitration point of the chapter.

**The verse reads:** "And those that came were male and female from all flesh, as God had commanded him. And the LORD shut him in."

The Hebrew of v. 16b — וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ — is a subject-change of maximum theological weight. The boarding narrative has been described using Elohim (כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים, "as God had commanded him," v. 16a). Then, at the moment of the door closing, the divine name switches to יְהוָה. The cosmic, ordered, taxonomic procession into the ark belongs to Elohim; the intimate act of sealing the door belongs to YHWH.

**Rendering of "shut him in":** All three agents agree on "shut him in" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) or "shut behind him" (EMET). The Hebrew preposition בַּעֲדוֹ carries both the spatial sense ("behind him") and the protective sense ("for him / on his behalf"). **Consensus adopts "shut him in"** (LOGOS, PNEUMA) — "shut him in" captures both the spatial sealing (he is now enclosed) and the protective enclosing (he is sealed in by divine care), whereas "shut behind him" is spatially accurate but loses the protective weight of בַּעֲדוֹ.

**Formatting decision:** PNEUMA formats the clause as a standalone sentence on its own line, set off by a paragraph break:

> *Then the LORD shut the door behind him.*

LOGOS renders it as a second clause of the same verse: "And the LORD shut him in." EMET maintains it in-verse.

**Consensus adopts a modified version of PNEUMA's formatting.** The clause is set on its own line as a standalone sentence with a preceding paragraph break, but without PNEUMA's additional word "door" (which is not in the Hebrew — the verb סָגַר takes no explicit object in the MT; the door is understood but not named). **Consensus renders:** "Then the LORD shut him in." — on its own line, preceded by a paragraph break (achieved by concluding the previous sentence with a period, as LOGOS does for v. 16a, and then the final clause stands alone).

The word "Then" (replacing EMET/LOGOS's "And") is adopted from PNEUMA's structural instinct: the narrative is not merely adding one more action but marking a shift. "Then" signals the temporal-structural turn — the loading is complete, and then, in this moment, YHWH acts. "Then" also carries more narrative weight than the continuous "And," appropriate for what PNEUMA rightly identifies as "the most tender moment in the chapter."

**The LXX (Vaticanus)** renders κύριος ὁ θεός ("Lord God") and adds ἔξωθεν ("from outside") — two expansions not in the MT. The consensus follows the MT: the subject is the LORD (YHWH) alone, without the added double-name; and the closing-from-outside is implied rather than stated, preserving the MT's compression and intimacy.

#### 7:17–18 — Waters lifting the ark; "prevailed"

The verb גָּבַר (gavar, "to prevail / to be strong") is used throughout vv. 18–20 and v. 24. **Consensus renders "prevailed" throughout** — consistent across all three agents and matching the GEN_006 note on this project's treatment of repeated verbal themes.

LOGOS: "bore up the ark"; EMET: "lifted the ark"; PNEUMA: "lifted the ark." **Consensus adopts "bore up the ark"** (LOGOS) for וַיִּשְׂאוּ אֶת-הַתֵּבָה — the verb נָשָׂא means "to carry/bear/lift," and "bore up" preserves the weight-bearing sense (the waters bore the ark's weight) that "lifted" slightly loses. "Bore up" also has a faintly elevated register that suits the verse's poetic accumulation.

EMET: "multiplied" for וַיִּרְבּוּ; LOGOS: "increased." **Consensus adopts "increased"** — it is more natural English for the gradual numerical augmentation of the waters. "Multiplied" is biological; "increased" is the right word for rising water.

#### 7:19–20 — The double מְאֹד מְאֹד; universal coverage

**7:19:** The doubled מְאֹד מְאֹד ("exceedingly exceedingly") is handled variously: EMET: "more and more"; LOGOS: "exceedingly greatly"; PNEUMA: "more and more, greatly." **Consensus adopts "more and more greatly"** — this preserves both intensifiers (the double מְאֹד) while avoiding the slightly awkward "exceedingly greatly" (LOGOS). "More and more greatly" captures the escalating sense of the double superlative and flows more naturally in English while remaining formally responsive to the Hebrew doubling.

**7:20:** "Fifteen cubits upward the waters prevailed, and the mountains were covered" — all three agents agree on the substance. **Consensus adopts this formulation.** "Upward" (מִלְמַעְלָה) is retained — it specifies the direction of measurement (the waters covered the mountains and then continued 15 cubits above them, measuring upward from the peak).

#### 7:21–23 — The death catalog: formatting decision; vocabulary

This is the chapter's second major formatting arbitration point.

**Formatting decision:** EMET renders vv. 21–23 as connected prose sentences. LOGOS renders them as prose with a single paragraph. PNEUMA formats them as broken verse, with each category on its own line:

> *And all flesh that moved on the earth perished —*
> *bird and animal and beast and every swarming creature that swarms on the earth,*
> *and every human being.*
>
> *Everything that had the breath of life in its nostrils —*
> *everything on dry land —*
> *died.*
>
> *He wiped out every living thing that was on the face of the ground:*
> *human being and animal, crawling creature and bird of the sky —*
> *they were wiped out from the earth.*
>
> *Only Noah remained — and those with him in the ark.*

**Consensus adopts the verse-formatted structure for vv. 21–23** with adjustments. The reasoning:

1. The Hebrew text does not mark these verses as poetry in the Tiberian cantillation system visible in the Leningrad Codex manuscripts, and they are not formatted as poetry in the Masoretic tradition. PNEUMA is correct that "the Hebrew repeats itself with deliberate insistence" and that the translator should not rush through this material — but the appropriate response to narrative weight is deliberate prose pacing, not the visual apparatus of verse.

2. However, PNEUMA's core insight is right: the death catalog does employ a structurally heightened rhetoric — the sustained catalogue, the repeated parallel clauses, the final restriction "Only Noah" — that distinguishes it from ordinary narrative prose. This is not ordinary reporting; it is liturgical enumeration of the dead.

3. **Consensus adopts a hybrid:** verse 21 is formatted with a dash suspension followed by the categories on their own line (as PNEUMA does); verse 22 is formatted with a two-line break structure (subject / condition / verb); verse 23 is formatted with its list on a separate line and "Only Noah" as its own final line. This preserves both the verse boundaries (maintaining the structure essential for a reference text) and the elevated rhetorical character of the passage, without fully converting it to poetry.

**Vocabulary — 7:21:**

The verb וַיִּגְוַע: "expired" (EMET) / "expired" (LOGOS) / "perished" (PNEUMA). PNEUMA's note is correct that גָּוַע (gava') is specifically physiological — it is the breath leaving a body, the word for expiring. EMET and LOGOS correctly render "expired." However, in context — the extinction of all terrestrial life — "expired" carries a slightly bureaucratic-clinical register in English. **Consensus adopts "expired"** for verse 21 — it is formally correct, and the clinical precision is appropriate: this is the medical fact of what happened to all flesh, stated without melodrama. The weight is in the catalog's accumulation, not in any single dramatic verb.

The categories in verse 21 follow PNEUMA's somewhat cleaner order ("bird and beast and living creature and every swarming thing"), but **consensus adjusts** to match the Hebrew sequence more closely: birds (עוֹף), beasts / livestock (בְּהֵמָה), living creatures (חַיָּה), swarming things (שֶׁרֶץ), and every human being (הָאָדָם). "Bird and beast and living creature and every swarming thing" — followed by "and every human being" as the climactic final category.

**Vocabulary — 7:22 — Compound breath formula (nishmat ruach chayyim):**

This is the chapter's most significant philological arbitration point.

The Hebrew is: כֹּל אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים בְּאַפָּיו — "all in whose nostrils was the nishmat-ruach-chayyim."

The phrase combines three elements in a construct chain:
- נִשְׁמָה (neshamah/nishmat) — the breath of Genesis 2:7, where YHWH breathed the נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים into the adam's nostrils
- רוּחַ (ruach) — spirit/wind/breath, the animating force of Genesis 1:2
- חַיִּים ("life")

The three translations handle this as follows:
- EMET: "the breath of the spirit of life" (preserving both terms in the construct chain)
- LOGOS: "the breath of the spirit of life" (same — LOGOS explicitly notes this preserves the compound chain over the LXX's collapsed πνοὴν ζωῆς)
- PNEUMA: "the breath of life in its nostrils" (collapses to the simpler phrase, echoing Gen. 2:7 directly but losing the ruach term)

**Consensus adopts "the breath of the spirit of life"** (EMET and LOGOS). Reasons:

1. The full compound נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים is the longest and most theologically elaborate breath-formula in the flood narrative. It is unique in the Hebrew Bible. To render it simply as "the breath of life" (as PNEUMA and the LXX do) is to collapse a compound construct chain into its simpler component, losing the explicit presence of רוּחַ (ruach). The loss is significant: ruach is the animating divine presence of Genesis 1:2. Both the neshamah of Genesis 2:7 and the ruach of Genesis 1:2 are simultaneously invoked at the moment of the great extinction. All that bore both the creation-breath and the creation-spirit died.

2. LOGOS's note that the NASB renders this "the breath of the spirit of life" and that this preserves the full construct chain is correct. The ESV and KJV's rendering of v. 22 as simply "the breath of life" follows the LXX's simplification rather than the MT's compound.

3. The three-part construct chain — neshamah / ruach / chayyim — is the single most theologically dense formula in the chapter and should not be compressed.

**"Everything on dry land" (מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר בֶּחָרָבָה):** All three agents preserve this phrase. The consensus renders it "everything on dry land" — the word חָרָבָה (charavah, "dry land") refers to the land that appeared when God gathered the waters in Genesis 1:9. The phrase marks the scope of the extinction: dry-land creatures only. Sea creatures are not mentioned.

**Vocabulary — 7:23:**

"He wiped out" (וַיִּמַח) — consistent with 7:4 and the GEN_006 ruling on מָחָה: "wipe out." The subject of the verb is grammatically ambiguous (third person masculine singular — God? The waters? The flood?). **Consensus adopts "He wiped out"** following PNEUMA and LOGOS, preserving the divine agency reading that the theological context strongly implies.

"Every living thing" (כָּל-הַיְקוּם) — see Textual Note below on יְקוּם.

The enumeration "from human being to beast, to creeping thing and to birds of the heavens" — this is the MT's order and all three agents preserve it. **Consensus follows this order.** Note that it descends from the highest category (human being) through intermediate (beast) to lower (creeping thing) and then back up to (birds) — this is approximately the reverse of the Genesis 1 creation sequence, with the exception that birds appear at the end rather than the beginning. The sequence mirrors 6:7's predicted de-creation order and is preserved exactly.

"And only Noah remained, and those who were with him in the ark" — **"Only Noah remained"** (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over PNEUMA's "Only Noah remained" (same). The particle אַךְ (akh, "only") is the particle of absolute restriction. **Consensus adopts "And only Noah remained"** — beginning with "And" preserves the Hebrew's narrative continuity after the wipe (וַיִּשָּׁאֶר, "and there remained"), and "only" carries the full weight of אַךְ.

#### 7:24 — "One hundred and fifty days"

All three agents agree: "the waters prevailed upon the earth one hundred and fifty days." **Consensus adopts this.** The verb גָּבַר ("prevailed") is consistent with vv. 18–20. The number is spelled out fully. This is the chapter's final statement — the silence of the prevailing waters before the turn of chapter 8.

---

## Key Wordplays and Literary Structures (Non-Translatable)

The following features are present in the Hebrew of Genesis 7 but cannot be reproduced in English. They are documented here for readers of the consensus translation.

### 1. The Distributive Reduplication (שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה / שְׁנַיִם שְׁנַיִם)

Hebrew uses number-reduplication to express "each" or "by [number]." In verse 2, שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה ("seven seven") = "seven pairs each" for the clean animals; in verses 9, 15, the entry formula שְׁנַיִם שְׁנַיִם ("two two") = "two by two." English resolves these to "seven pairs" and "two by two" respectively, losing the surface-level numerical reduplication but preserving the distributive sense.

### 2. The YHWH/Elohim Alternation — Source-Seam Theology

Genesis 7 contains one of the most studied divine-name alternations in the Pentateuch:
- יְהוָה (the LORD): verses 1, 5, 16b
- אֱלֹהִים (God): verses 9, 16a

The Documentary Hypothesis identifies this alternation as a seam between the Yahwist (J) and Priestly (P) sources. The consensus translation preserves the alternation exactly, making no attempt to harmonize. The theologically charged result — that in verse 16, the same verse uses both names, with Elohim governing the orderly boarding and YHWH performing the intimate closure — is a feature of the received canonical text, whatever its compositional history. The LXX (Vaticanus) erases this by using κύριος ὁ θεός ("the Lord God") at v. 16b — the consensus follows the MT in keeping the names distinct and the moment sharp.

### 3. The Adam / Ground (אָדָם / אֲדָמָה) Echo

The word for "human being" (אָדָם) and the word for "ground" (אֲדָמָה) continue their Primeval History resonance throughout chapter 7. The flood is announced as wiping "every living thing from upon the face of the ground (אֲדָמָה)" (v. 4), and the death of the adam (v. 21) is the death of the creature whose name is the ground's name. The adam returns into the adamah — but not through death's natural return to the dust (Genesis 3:19), but through the flood's total erasure. The un-creation mirrors the creation vocabulary.

### 4. The Breath Echo — Genesis 2:7 / Genesis 7:22

The phrase נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים ("the breath of the spirit of life") in verse 22 reaches back to Genesis 2:7: וַיִּפַּח בְּאַפָּיו נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים ("and he breathed into his nostrils the breath of life"). The neshamah that was breathed in at the creation of the adam is the neshamah that is extinguished in every nostril at the flood. What God breathed in, the flood breathes out.

### 5. The Wipe (מָחָה) — Promise and Fulfillment

The verb מָחָה ("to wipe out") was announced in Genesis 6:7 (אֶמְחֶה — "I will wipe out") and threatened again in 7:4 (וּמָחִיתִי — "and I will wipe out"). In verse 23, the promise is kept: וַיִּמַח — "and he wiped out." The same verb at future, future, and perfect constitutes its own narrative arc: announcement, reiteration, completion. The word carries the whole weight of the prophetic-fulfillment structure of the flood narrative.

### 6. The Ark (תֵּבָה) — Noah and Moses

The word תֵּבָה (tevah, "ark") is used in the Hebrew Bible only for the Noahic ark (Genesis 6–9) and for the basket in which the infant Moses was placed in the Nile (Exodus 2:3, 5). Both tevah vessels float on waters of death and carry the sole surviving bearer of divine promise to safety. The structural parallel between Noah and Moses — between the great flood and the Nile — is one of the most deliberate literary connections in the Pentateuch.

### 7. The Remnant (אַךְ נֹחַ) — Prototype of the Shearit

"Only Noah" (אַךְ נֹחַ) in verse 23 is the prototypical statement of the shearit — the surviving remnant — that becomes the central theological concept of the Hebrew prophets (Isaiah 10:20–22; Micah 5:7; Zephaniah 3:13). The flood narrative is the founding instance of the remnant-theology: from the total annihilation of an old world, one family survives to constitute the seed of a new one. The structure of judgment-and-remnant that the prophets apply to Israel's national history is first enacted here.

---

## Textual Notes by Verse

### 7:1 — בֹּא (bo') — "Come"

The imperative of בּוֹא ("to come/enter"), second person singular, is literally "come" rather than "go." It has been argued (LOGOS Translation Note #11) that this implies the LORD speaks from within the ark — inviting Noah to join him in the place of safety rather than ordering him toward a destination. The grammatical observation is correct; the theological inference is an ancient and significant homiletical tradition (Rashi, and others). The translation "Come" preserves this possibility without enforcing it.

### 7:2 — אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ (ish ve'ishto) — "a male and his female"

Literally "a man and his woman/wife." The vocabulary of intimate human pairing is applied to animals. The LXX (Vaticanus) substitutes ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ ("male and female") — the biological binary without relational idiom. The MT's choice of the intimate vocabulary is deliberate and is preserved in the consensus translation.

### 7:4, 7:23 — יְקוּם (yequm) — "living thing"

The rare word יְקוּם derives from קוּם ("to stand/rise"), meaning literally "that which stands" — upright, living creatures. It appears four times in the Hebrew Bible (Gen. 7:4, 7:23; Num. 16:30; Deut. 11:6), always in contexts of total destruction. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ἀνάστημα ("standing thing, that which stands upright") at 7:23 — a literal reflection of the Hebrew's meaning. The consensus renders יְקוּם as "living thing" — the most natural English equivalent for the concept (living creatures that stand/exist). EMET's "standing-thing" is more etymologically precise but is not established English usage.

### 7:11 — בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה (be'etsem hayyom hazzeh) — "on that very day"

The phrase uses עֶצֶם ("bone/substance/very-self") as an intensifier: "on the very bone/substance of this day." The idiom marks the most solemn calendrical moments in the Hebrew Bible: the Exodus from Egypt (Exodus 12:17, 12:41, 12:51), the death of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:48), and the entry into the ark (Genesis 7:13). The precise date — second month, seventeenth day — is confirmed in both Leningrad Codex folios.

### 7:11 — תְּהוֹם רַבָּה (tehom rabbah) — "the great deep"

The word תְּהוֹם (tehom) is the primordial pre-creation deep of Genesis 1:2 — the chaotic waters over which the Spirit of God hovered before creation. Its cognate in Babylonian mythology is Tiamat, the primordial sea. The flood text does not mythologize the deep, but the cosmological resonance of the term is intentional: the same tehom that was bounded by creation is now erupting through it. The LXX renders ἄβυσσος (abyss) — the word that enters English as "abyss" and reappears in the New Testament (Revelation 9:1, 11:7, 20:1) as the realm of the demonic and uncreated.

### 7:13 — Shem, Ham, Japheth

The sons are named in the order in which they consistently appear throughout the flood narrative. The name order may reflect birth order, but Genesis 9–10 will reverse the order of prominence (Shem elevated, Ham dishonored). The names are preserved as received English transliterations.

### 7:16 — וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ (vayyisghor YHWH ba'ado) — "Then the LORD shut him in"

The verb סָגַר (sagar) means "to shut, to close, to seal." The preposition בַּעֲד combines spatial-behind with protective-on-behalf-of: both "behind him" and "for him." Classical source criticism (Wellhausen) identifies this clause as a Yahwist (J) interpolation into a Priestly (P) boarding narrative, the subject-change being a compositional seam. Whether that analysis is correct, the canonical text uses the divine-name change at this precise moment with unmistakable theological effect: the personal, covenantal name of God performs the most personal, covenantal act in the chapter.

### 7:22 — נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים (nishmat-ruach chayyim) — "the breath of the spirit of life"

See Wordplay #4 above. This three-term construct chain is unique in the Hebrew Bible. EMET, LOGOS, and the consensus render it in full. The LXX collapses it to πνοὴν ζωῆς ("breath of life") — a two-word phrase that loses the explicit ruach. The NASB renders "breath of the spirit of life," following the full chain. The ESV and KJV render "breath of life" at this verse, following the LXX's simplification.

### 7:23 — Subject ambiguity of וַיִּמַח (vayyimach)

The verb is third person masculine singular with no explicit stated subject. Both EMET and LOGOS note this ambiguity — is the subject God or the waters (described in the previous verses as the active agent of destruction)? EMET observes: "The ambiguity may be deliberate: the judgment is both divine act and natural catastrophe; the waters wipe as YHWH wipes." The consensus renders "He wiped out" — the most natural reading of a third-person verb in a context where God has been the explicit agent of the flood from verse 4 onward. The ambiguity is noted here and need not be resolved in the main text.

### 7:24 — חֲמִשִּׁים וּמְאַת יוֹם (chamishim ume'at yom) — "one hundred and fifty days"

150 days = 5 months × 30 days, corresponding to the period between the 17th day of the 2nd month (7:11) and the 17th day of the 7th month (8:4), when the ark rested on Ararat. The flood narrative uses a 30-day month calendar (the ancient Near Eastern administrative calendar). The arithmetic is precise and consistent throughout Genesis 7–8. The 150-day period of the prevailing waters is the zenith of the de-creation, before the turn of 8:1: "And God remembered Noah."

---

## LXX (Vaticanus) Comparison — Genesis 7

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Consensus Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7:1 | בֹּא ("Come") | εἴσελθε ("Enter") | "Come" — MT imperative preserved; theological implication of the LORD inviting from within retained |
| 7:2 | אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ ("a man and his woman") | ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ ("male and female") | "a male and his female" — MT's intimate relational vocabulary retained over LXX's biological binary |
| 7:2 | שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה ("seven seven") | ἑπτὰ ἑπτά ("seven seven") | "seven pairs" — distributive interpreted for English; LXX also preserves distributive literally |
| 7:11 | מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה ("fountains of the great deep") | πηγαὶ τῆς ἀβύσσου ("springs of the abyss") | "fountains of the great deep" — MT's cosmological scale preferred over LXX's "springs"; both are defensible |
| 7:11 | אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם ("windows of the heavens") | καταρράκται τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ("floodgates/cataracts of heaven") | "windows of the heavens" — MT's architectural-cosmological image retained; LXX's hydraulic "floodgates" noted |
| 7:16 | וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה ("and the LORD shut") | καὶ ἔκλεισεν κύριος ὁ θεός ("and the Lord God shut") | "Then the LORD shut him in" — MT's single-name YHWH preserved; LXX's double-name expansion and added "from outside" not adopted |
| 7:19 | גָּבְרוּ מְאֹד מְאֹד ("prevailed exceedingly exceedingly") | ὑπερεπλεόνασεν ("super-abounded") | "prevailed more and more greatly" — MT's double intensifier preserved; LXX compound verb noted |
| 7:22 | נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים ("breath of spirit of life") | πνοὴν ζωῆς ("breath of life") | "the breath of the spirit of life" — MT's compound construct chain preserved; LXX's simplified two-term formula not adopted |
| 7:23 | וַיִּשָּׁאֶר אַךְ-נֹחַ ("there remained only Noah") | κατελείφθη μόνος Νωε ("Noah alone was left behind") | "And only Noah remained" — MT's active verb + restriction particle preserved; LXX's passive noted |
